title.emphasis: Part II: A New Jurisprudence—The Prostitution-Miller Test

section.para: To construct a coherent legal framework, this report proposes the Prostitution-Miller Test, a novel analytical tool that adapts the three-prong standard from the Supreme Court's obscenity jurisprudence in Miller v. California. An act fails to be criminal prostitution if it satisfies any one of the following conditions, thereby establishing its legality.

title.emphasis: 2.1 Prong 3: The "Serious Work" Standard (Lacks Serious Value)

section.para:
The constitutional gateway for this analysis is the California Supreme Court's decision in
People v. Freeman (1988).13 In that landmark case, the court reversed the pandering conviction of a producer who hired and paid actors to perform explicit sexual acts for a non-obscene film.13 The court's reasoning was that the payments were "acting fees" for creating a work of expression, not compensation paid for the purpose of sexual arousal or gratification.14

This decision establishes a powerful legal principle: a compensated sexual act that possesses "serious artistic value" (by being part of a non-obscene film) is constitutionally protected expression and cannot be prosecuted as prostitution.13 This "serious work" standard is the key to legality.

title.emphasis: 2.2 Prong 1: The Substantial Purpose Test (Prurient Interest)

section.para: This prong expands the "serious work" concept to other non-prurient purposes. A compensated sexual act undertaken for a substantial purpose other than appealing to a "shameful or morbid interest in sex" falls outside the rationale of prostitution laws.1 California law provides powerful, real-world examples of such licit contracts:

listitem.para: Compensated Gestational Surrogacy: California has a comprehensive legal framework (Family Code §§ 7960-7962) that regulates and enforces complex agreements involving payment for the use of a person's body for the serious purpose of procreation.1 This demonstrates the state's ability to distinguish a lawful contract for bodily services from a criminal act.1

Surrogate Partner Therapy: This practice exists in a legal gray area but presents a potentially lawful agreement for sexual acts with a therapeutic purpose. Structured correctly under the framework for unlicensed healing arts and with full disclosure, such an agreement's dominant motive is therapeutic, not gratificational.1

Parentage by Agreement: California's Uniform Parentage Act allows for the creation of complex parenting agreements when a child is conceived through sexual intercourse, including recognizing three-parent families.3 These agreements, which manage the legal outcomes of a sexual act, are another example of enforceable contracts in the intimate sphere.3

title.emphasis: 2.3 Prong 2: The Affirmative Consent Standard (Patently Offensive)

section.para: This prong connects the concept of "patently offensive" conduct directly to the principles of affirmative consent.1 In the context of a personal service, an act becomes "patently offensive" precisely when it violates the provider's autonomy. Therefore, the failure to secure and maintain ongoing, enthusiastic, and freely given affirmative consent is what makes the conduct offensive and unlawful. This creates a

duty of care under California Civil Code §1714(a), which holds individuals responsible for injuries caused by their actions.1 A reckless disregard for consent constitutes a breach of this duty, creating potential tort liability in addition to any criminal liability.1
